summary: The author placed 10th in a time series competition focused on predicting and ranking 400 commodity spreads daily. Recognizing that the short ~3-month test window and synthetic removal of carry effects made complex ML signals unlearnable, they pivoted to a theoretically grounded, regularized naive approach. The method leverages a covariance matrix regularized against validation sets of similar size to the private test, combined with Kelly-optimal portfolio ranking using standardized daily ranks. This simple, robust strategy outperformed more complex ML attempts.
extensive_summary:
The competition required predicting and ranking 400 commodity spreads daily, with targets structured as return-based spreads grouped across different time horizons. Initially drawn to the setup by pairs cointegration concepts, the author quickly realized the task differed fundamentally: the targets were return-based, the test period was only ~3 months, and the provided price series appeared to have synthetically removed carry effects. These constraints led to the insight that no ML-usable signal was likely present, and any non-optimal prediction would degrade performance rapidly in such a short window.

Instead of pursuing complex models, the author adopted a carefully regularized naive approach. The theoretical foundation relies on the in-sample optimal prediction being rank-equivalent to a Kelly optimal portfolio, implemented via Kelly-leverage using standardized ranks. For out-of-sample prediction, the covariance matrix was regularized using validation sets sized similarly to the private test period. Preprocessing steps included forward-filling NaNs in prices before target construction and performing daily rank standardization. While one submission excluded the 2020-2021 period, the final submission used the full 2018-2025 dataset and achieved a score of 0.479, aligning with late validation scores around 0.4. The writeup emphasizes that in highly constrained, short-horizon time series tasks, a robust, theoretically informed naive method often outperforms complex machine learning pipelines.
competition_flow: Raw price data was forward-filled for NaNs, used to construct return-based spread targets, standardized daily ranks were computed, and a covariance matrix regularized against a ~3-month validation set was applied to generate Kelly-optimal portfolio rankings for the final submission.
data_processing: Forward-filling NaNs in prices before target construction
Covariance matrix regularization using validation sets matching the private test size
Exclusion of the 2020-2021 period in one attempt (full 2018-2025 used for final)
cv_strategy: Holdout validation sets sized similarly to the private test period (~3 months).
ensembling: No ensembling or post-processing was applied; the submission consisted of a single regularized naive prediction.
insights: In short-horizon time series competitions, complex ML signals are often unlearnable and degrade performance quickly.
A theoretically grounded, regularized naive approach can be more robust and effective than complex models.
The optimal in-sample prediction aligns with a Kelly-optimal portfolio using standardized ranks.
critical_findings: The test period was only ~3 months, making it too short for small statistical edges to beat random chance.
The provided price series appeared to have synthetically removed carry effects in a non-straightforward way.
Targets were return-based spreads rather than price-based, and the ranking involved spreads across different time horizons.
what_did_not_work: Complex ML models, which were explored but confirmed to be unlearnable for this task.
